quote: one of our current customers, Tyson Jones, has said that you might be interested in solar for your home
stewart_read: Holland opens with a referral attribution — 'Tyson Jones referred you.' This lead type (referral from existing customer) does not appear in the codex's lead taxonomy, which covers paid, organic, and recycled leads only. The referral framing is doing real legitimacy work here — Mia's immediate warm response ('Yes. Uh-huh.') is partly a function of the named referral. The codex has no guidance on how to handle referral leads, whether bill collection is required, or how to leverage the referral relationship in the pitch.
alternative_hypothesis: The referral could be treated as a subtype of organic lead for bill-collection purposes — but that is an assumption, not a documented rule.
coaching_implication: Flag for Kenny: referral leads are a distinct lead type not covered by the current codex taxonomy. Manager should confirm whether referral leads follow organic-lead bill rules (bill preferred, not required) and whether the referral relationship should be leveraged more explicitly in the pitch (e.g., 'Tyson mentioned your bill situation'). This is a codex gap, not a rep gap.

quote: I see the pool back there too. That's fun. Do you guys use that pool quite a bit?
stewart_read: Holland spots the pool via satellite imagery and asks about usage — a genuine load-signal probe. The customer confirms heavy use ('Yes.'). This is a material qualifier: a pool running through Virginia summers is a significant electricity draw that compounds the already-high $500-600 bill. Holland notices the signal but does not anchor it to bill impact. The pool mention disappears from the conversation entirely after this exchange.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been saving the pool detail for the closer's design conversation — a strategic deferral. But there is no evidence of this; she simply moves on to the next verify item.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, show Holland how to bridge the pool observation to bill pain: the pool is already in the $500-600 bill — solar doesn't just cut the house load, it covers the pool load too. That bridge makes the bill flip land harder. The signal was noticed; the anchor was missed.

quote: Oh, I know. They've been getting out of hand lately. I totally understand.
stewart_read: Mia volunteers 'That electricity bill is so dang on high' at 01:45 — the call's highest-energy moment. Holland's response is empathetic but passive: she validates the pain and moves on. The codex's capture-and-flip pattern requires the bill to be turned into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. Holland captures; she does not flip. The $500-600 figure Mia provides at 02:11 is the number that should have been weaponized — 'That's $6,000 a year going to Rappahannock' — but Holland instead pivots to the program pitch.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been intentionally deferring the flip to avoid over-pitching before the credit qualifier — keeping the call moving. But the flip does not require a pitch; it is a one-sentence reframe that costs no time.
coaching_implication: This is the floor-wide coaching gap the codex flags: every rep captures the bill and none flip it. Show Holland the inversion move: take Mia's own words ('so dang on high') and redirect them at Rappahannock, not at solar. The emotional work is already done — Holland just needs to point it in the right direction.

quote: it guarantees you savings within the first year you have solar. Just as long as you have a credit score above 6 70, then you guys will qualify.
stewart_read: Holland states the credit threshold as 670 — matching the codex's documented threshold exactly. This is a positive schema-compliance data point. The codex flags SESSION18_fcbf33c1 where a rep stated 650 instead of 670, raising a floor-wide drift question. This call confirms Holland is using the correct number. Worth noting for the manager's floor-wide threshold-consistency tracking.
coaching_implication: No coaching action needed for Holland on this specific point. Use this call as a positive data point in the floor-wide credit-threshold drift conversation with Kenny — Holland is on the right number; the SESSION18 rep was not.

quote: So now the last thing that we need to do is just find a good time that my specialist can stop by and walk you through through that design.
stewart_read: Holland says 'stop by' — framing the appointment as an in-person visit. The codex's button_up protocol specifies a phone appointment with explicit 'not driving, at computer' anchors. If the closer is actually calling by phone, the customer may be confused or unprepared. Holland also omits the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake and the 'no driving, at computer' anchor — two required button_up elements. The appointment is booked but the confirmation protocol is incomplete.
alternative_hypothesis: Ion may conduct in-person closer visits in some markets — 'stop by' could be accurate. But the codex specifies phone appointment as the standard, and no in-person variant is documented.
coaching_implication: Walk Holland through the full button_up checklist: phone vs in-person framing, 'not driving and at computer' anchor, and the 'is that fair?' handshake. Each omitted element is a no-show risk. The 'stop by' language is the most urgent fix — if the closer calls by phone and the customer expected an in-person visit, the sit is at risk before it starts.

quote: I'll just send, Mia a a text of your guys' appointment details so you can add it to your calendar as well.
stewart_read: Holland sends the confirmation text to Mia only, despite the husband being on the call and on the title. The codex's spouse_decision protocol requires both decision-makers to be present and engaged in the appointment. Sending confirmation to one spouse only creates a single point of failure — if Mia doesn't relay the details, the husband may not show. The husband was included on the call but is not anchored into the appointment confirmation.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may only have Mia's number and no practical way to text the husband separately. But she could have verbally confirmed the appointment with the husband directly before hanging up.
coaching_implication: Show Holland the close-the-loop move: before hanging up, address the husband directly — 'And you'll both be available Monday at 5?' — and confirm he has the details. The text going to Mia only is a structural vulnerability on a two-decision-maker appointment.

quote: Can you hold on? Can you hold can I put my husband on the phone? Hold on one second. Yeah. No problem.
stewart_read: Mia self-initiates adding her husband mid-pitch — a positive signal that she wants him involved. Holland's 'Yeah. No problem.' is the correct response: she does not push through the qualifier without him, does not argue, and waits through the hold. This is protocol-compliant spouse handling. The codex's positive example (SESSION18_fd078269) shows the rep framing the appointment as the spouse-inclusion venue; here the customer does that work herself. Holland's job was to not break it — she didn't.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland could have been more proactive — she had already surfaced the husband at 01:28 (title question) and could have suggested including him before starting the program pitch. The customer beat her to it.
coaching_implication: Positive reinforcement moment: Holland handled the hold correctly. In the 1-on-1, acknowledge this and then layer the proactive version — once the title question surfaces a spouse, the rep can front-run the inclusion: 'Before I walk you through the program, would it make sense to have him on the line?' That prevents the mid-pitch interruption and the ~90-second hold.
